channel.title: Robert Akhmerov | v31dt
channel.description: Recent content on Robert Akhmerov | v31dt

item.title: About Me
item.description:
I am Robert Akhmerov, also known online as v31dt. I study Applied Computer Science at Howest, majoring in Cybersecurity, and I am currently building toward a junior penetration tester or security engineering role.

My work focuses on practical security: web and mobile penetration testing, Burp Suite workflows, Android security assessment, malware analysis, reverse engineering, and automation. During my offensive security internship at KBC, I worked on mobile penetration testing automation, Burp Suite and Frida integration, OWASP MASVS-based assessment, and Android banking malware research.

item.title: How I Built This Portfolio
item.description:
This portfolio is built with Hugo, a static site generator. I chose Hugo because it keeps the portfolio simple, fast, and easy to maintain. Each article is written as a Markdown file, and Hugo converts those files into a static website.

The visual theme is Archie, a minimal Hugo theme. I selected it because it keeps the focus on the content: reflections, projects, and proof of professional development.

The source code is hosted in the GitHub repository v31dt/v31dt.github.io. GitHub Pages publishes the repository from the main branch. The generated Hugo output is committed in the repository root so the current GitHub Pages configuration can serve the website directly.

item.title: KBC Offensive Security Internship
item.description:
Context

From February 2026 to May 2026, I completed an offensive security internship at KBC. The work focused on mobile penetration testing, Android banking malware research, and automation for security testing environments.

Technologies

  • Burp Suite
  • Frida
  • OWASP MASVS
  • Android security testing
  • Malware analysis tooling
  • Automation scripting

My Contribution

I developed automation for mobile penetration testing environments, integrated Burp Suite with Frida for login flow automation, contributed to a KBC mobile security assessment based on OWASP MASVS, and researched Android banking malware.

item.title: Cybersec Europe 2026
item.description:
Cybersec Europe 2026 took place at Brussels Expo on 20 and 21 May 2026. It is one of the largest cybersecurity events in the Benelux, with a mix of vendors, talks, technical sessions, demos, and networking opportunities. I attended it with friends from my cybersecurity circle: Artūrs Jakovļevs, Rostyslav Fedorov, Fedor Vinogradov, and Vladyslav Rovnyi.

The target audience was broad but still very relevant for me: security professionals, students, vendors, CISOs, engineers, and people working around governance, cloud, identity, data protection, and threat intelligence. That mix made the event useful because it did not feel limited to one narrow area of security. There were technical topics, but also discussions about strategy, policy, resilience, and how organisations should think about security at scale.

item.title: Future of Bug Bounty and How to Build Your Own HackBot
item.description:
For my Professional Networking podcast assignment, I spoke with Pedro Paniago, Offensive Security Manager at PwC Belgium, bug hunter, and a distinct voice in Belgium’s security scene.

Topic

The episode focuses on the future of bug bounty, AI’s influence on bug hunting, and how security researchers can build their own AI hackbot from scratch.

Guest

Pedro Paniago is an Offensive Security Manager at PwC Belgium. Besides his professional work, he is active in bug hunting and regularly shares a practical view on offensive security, automation, and the changing role of AI in cybersecurity.

item.title: Creativity Needs Structure
item.description:
This is the second part of a connected pair of posts. The first one is about learning through systems.

I used to think about creativity as something that happens when structure disappears. The more freedom, the more original the result. That sounds attractive, but I do not think it is true anymore.

Total freedom is often not creative. It can become vague. If there are no constraints, there is nothing to push against, no direction, and no reason to choose one path over another. A blank page can look like freedom, but it can also be a trap.

item.title: Learning Through Systems
item.description:
This is the first part of a connected pair of posts. This one is about learning. The second one is about creativity.

I do not really believe in relying on motivation as the main engine for getting good. Motivation is useful, but it is unstable. Some days it is there, some days it is not. A system is different. A system gives you a way to keep moving even when the mood is average, the task is repetitive, or the result is not immediately visible.

item.title: Insecure Deserialization Research Workflow
item.description:
Insecure deserialization doesn’t get the attention it deserves.

Finding the sink is one thing, but the actual painful part is usually exploitability: building the gadget / POP chain and proving the payload actually works in that specific runtime. That’s the part that used to eat most of the researcher time.

I think AI opens up a pretty big opportunity here. Not really as some magic “generate exploit” button, but to automate a lot of the chain discovery process: finding reachable gadgets, killing dead paths, reasoning about object/property constraints, and validating candidates.

item.title: OAuth 2.1 as an Attack Surface
item.description:
OAuth is such a good attack surface because there is simply so much that can go wrong during implementation.

With OAuth 2.1 moving forward, and MCP authorization building on top of it, I think this space is going to get a lot of attention again.

The research angle is not only PKCE becoming mandatory or older flows being removed, but also the new client metadata / CIMD-style flows. When authorization servers start fetching client metadata URLs and resolving fields like logo_uri or jwks_uri, you get a fresh set of SSRF and trust-boundary issues to look at.

item.title: The 5 Levels of Infrastructure as Code
item.description:
On 28 April 2026, I attended the Howest Tech&Meet “The 5 Levels of Infrastructure as Code” at Howest Campus Brugge Station. The session was given by Tim Van Roosbroeck and Dimitry Decan from Orbid, and focused on how organisations move from manual cloud configuration toward mature, governed Infrastructure as Code.

I wanted to attend this talk because infrastructure is one of the areas where mistakes can become expensive very quickly. In security, it is easy to focus on application bugs or malware, but badly managed infrastructure can create just as much risk. Misconfigured access, forgotten resources, inconsistent environments, and manual changes are all problems that attackers can benefit from.

item.title: PQC: How to Prepare for the Quantum Era
item.description: On 24 March 2026, I attended the Howest Tech&Meet “PQC: How to Prepare for the Quantum Era” at Howest Campus Brugge Station. The speaker was Sarah Ampe, Digital Risk Manager at EY Belgium, who works around cryptography, PKI, and post-quantum readiness. The session focused on what happens when current public-key cryptography becomes vulnerable to quantum computing, and what organisations can already do before that becomes an emergency.

item.title: CVE-2026-25471: WordPress Admin Safety Guard Authentication Bypass
item.description:
Context

I discovered and responsibly disclosed CVE-2026-25471, a high-severity authentication bypass vulnerability in the WordPress Admin Safety Guard plugin by Themepaste. The issue affects vulnerable versions of the plugin up to the versions listed in the public advisory, and it was published by Patchstack in March 2026.

The vulnerable functionality was part of the plugin’s administrator login protection flow. That made the bug more interesting than a normal WordPress issue: the plugin was installed to make admin access safer, but the way it handled the OTP verification path created a separate route into the authentication process.

item.title: CSCBE 2026 ECSC Track Finalist
item.description:
Context

In February 2026, I ranked 8th nationally in the Cyber Security Challenge Belgium Junior track qualifiers and was selected for the ECSC track finals.

Reflection

The competition gave me a useful benchmark outside school. It tested speed, problem solving, and technical range, while also connecting me more directly with the Belgian cybersecurity community.

item.title: DeepSeek Uncovered: The Open-Source AI Challenger from the East
item.description: On 9 December 2025, I attended the Howest Tech&Meet “DeepSeek Uncovered: The Open-Source AI Challenger from the East” at Howest Campus Brugge Station. The session was given by Dimitri Casier, lecturer in Software Development and AI at Howest. It focused on DeepSeek, why it became such a visible name in the AI discussion, and how open-source AI models compare with more closed systems from companies like OpenAI and Anthropic.

item.title: The Birth of NATO's Cyber Defence
item.description: On 2 December 2025, I attended the Howest Tech&Meet “The Birth of NATO’s Cyber Defence: Insights from an Insider” at Howest Campus Brugge Station. The speaker was Martin De Pauw, who has worked in military technology, networks, IT, and cyber within the NATO context. The talk focused on how NATO’s view of cyber changed over time, especially around 2016, when cyberspace was officially recognised as an operational domain next to land, sea, and air.

item.title: Hack The Future: AquaRadar
item.description:
Hack The Future was a good reminder that a hackathon is not only about having a good idea. It is also about knowing what you already have, and not throwing that advantage away once the pressure starts.

Hackathon

  • Name: Hack The Future
  • Challenge: Gotta Catch ‘Em All
  • Project: AquaRadar
  • Focus: a Pokemon-like fish spotting game

Reflection

The challenge was called “Gotta Catch ‘Em All”. The idea was simple and fun: build something like a Pokemon-style game, but instead of catching fictional creatures, users would spot real fish. Our version became AquaRadar, a system where someone could upload an image, get a result, review it, and slowly build up a small memory of what had already been recognised.

item.title: Threat Intelligence: from Threats to Tactics
item.description:
On 7 October 2025, I attended the Howest Tech event “Threat Intelligence: from Threats to Tactics”. The event focused on preparing organisations for modern cyber threats and translating threat knowledge into practical decisions. The session was physical, ran from 19:00 to 21:00, and fit very closely with the way I want to grow as a cybersecurity student.

Threat intelligence is one of the areas I am genuinely interested in because it is directly applicable to the work I already enjoy: bug bounty, offensive security, malware analysis, and threat-centred thinking. A lot of beginner security work starts from a checklist: test this endpoint, try this payload, scan this surface. That has value, but it can become mechanical. Threat intelligence adds context. It asks who might attack, what they usually do, what infrastructure or techniques they rely on, and which signals matter before an incident becomes obvious.

item.title: AInalyzer
item.description:
Context

AInalyzer is an open-source AI-powered Burp Suite extension. It integrates an AI assistant into HTTP endpoint security investigations while keeping a human in the loop.

Technologies

  • Burp Suite extension development
  • AI-assisted analysis
  • HTTP security testing
  • Human-in-the-loop workflows

Reflection

AInalyzer reflects my interest in combining AI with practical security work. The main idea is not to replace the tester, but to make investigation faster while keeping human judgement in control.

item.title: ELK + Burp Suite Monitoring System
item.description:
Context

This project built a Burp Suite and ELK Stack setup for centralized storage, analysis, and anomaly detection of HTTP requests.

Technologies

  • Burp Suite
  • Elasticsearch
  • Logstash / Beats
  • Kibana
  • Filebeat
  • HTTP request analysis

Result

The setup supports security research and vulnerability tracking by collecting and analyzing HTTP traffic in a centralized monitoring stack.

Reflection

This project connected penetration testing workflows with monitoring and analysis. It pushed me to think beyond individual findings and look at how request data can be stored, searched, and used during longer security investigations.
